mozart-figaro-1786.work: Premiere of The Marriage of Figaro
mozart-figaro-1786.context: On 1 May 1786, Mozart's opera The Marriage of Figaro premiered at Vienna's Imperial Court Theatre (on the site of today's Burgtheater). Lorenzo Da Ponte adapted the libretto from Beaumarchais's satirical comedy — a play the Austrian court had banned outright for its attack on aristocratic privilege. Mozart turned this class struggle into a sublime musical comedy; Emperor Joseph II attended the premiere, and repeated encores nearly doubled the performance's length.
mozart-figaro-1786.meaning: The opera wraps political critique in laughter, letting the coming revolutionary mood fill the hall. It is both the summit of Viennese Classical opera and a portent of the tension between Enlightenment ideals and the old order — three years later, the French Revolution erupted. With it Mozart established Vienna as the most important operatic capital of 18th-century Europe.

place.name: Burgtheater, Vienna (site of the former Imperial Court Theatre)
place.note: Today's Burgtheater was built in 1888; the original Court Theatre where the premiere took place has been demolished, so the coordinates are approximate.

source.summary: K. 492, premiered 1 May 1786 at Vienna's Imperial Court Theatre, libretto by Da Ponte.

listening.note: Try the Overture and the Act II finale to hear how Mozart distils class conflict into musical essence.

beethoven-eroica-1804.context: In the summer of 1804, Beethoven gave the private premiere of his Third Symphony at the Lobkowitz Palace in Vienna. The work had been dedicated to Napoleon, but when Beethoven learned that Napoleon had crowned himself Emperor, he furiously scratched out the dedication on the title page. At nearly fifty minutes it overturned the scale of Classical form, and its two opening dissonant chords stunned the Viennese audience.
beethoven-eroica-1804.meaning: The 'Eroica' is a watershed in the history of Western symphonic music. It declared that heroism no longer belonged to nobles and emperors but to the human spirit itself. Beethoven turned Vienna into a laboratory for a new musical language, and his daring gave direct birth to the Romantic era.

place.name: Lobkowitz Palace
place.note: Prince Lobkowitz was an important patron of Beethoven; several major works were privately premiered here.

source.summary: Op. 55, privately premiered around summer 1804 at the Lobkowitz Palace; the public premiere was on 7 April 1805.

listening.note: Try the first movement and the second-movement 'Funeral March' to feel Beethoven's extreme reading of heroism and death.

schubert-winterreise-1827.context: In 1827, ailing with syphilis and steadily failing, Schubert completed his darkest vocal masterpiece, Winterreise — a cycle of 24 songs for tenor and piano set to poems by Wilhelm Müller. That November he performed the cycle privately for friends at the apartment of his old friend Franz von Schober; they were baffled by its gloom, and only Schubert himself was deeply satisfied.
schubert-winterreise-1827.meaning: Winterreise is Romantic music's most profound gaze into loneliness, loss and death. Schubert died the following year, making the work feel like a final farewell to Vienna — the city he both loved and felt suffocated by.

place.name: Schubert's last residence (Kettenbrückengasse 6)
place.note: Schubert died here in November 1828; Winterreise was completed shortly before. It is now a Schubert memorial museum.

source.summary: D. 911, completed 1827, poems by Wilhelm Müller, 24 songs; the last private performance Schubert took part in was around November 1827.

listening.note: Try the first song 'Gute Nacht' and the twenty-fourth, 'Der Leiermann', to feel the fated circle from the journey's start to its end.

strauss-blue-danube-1867.context: On 15 February 1867, The Blue Danube (An der schönen blauen Donau) premiered at the Sofiensaal in Vienna, performed by the Vienna Men's Choral Association. Sung with a text, the premiere met a tepid response; only weeks later, as a purely orchestral version at the Paris World's Fair, did it cause a sensation. After Austria's defeat in the Austro-Prussian War the country badly needed to rebuild its national confidence, and this waltz arrived at exactly the right moment.
strauss-blue-danube-1867.meaning: The Blue Danube gradually became the spiritual emblem of Vienna and of Austria itself. Played as the encore at the Vienna New Year's Concert each year, it is a ritual summoning of an elegant memory of the lost imperial age. It raised the Danube from a geographical fact into a vessel for collective emotion.

place.name: Sofiensaal (former site)
place.note: The Sofiensaal was destroyed in WWII; the site has since been rebuilt for other uses, so the coordinates are approximate.

source.summary: Op. 314, premiered 15 February 1867 at the Vienna Sofiensaal; the original version had a choral text by Josef Weyl.

listening.note: Try Karajan's recording with the Vienna Philharmonic to hear this waltz's perfect evocation of imperial elegance.

mahler-das-lied-1909.context: Between 1907 and 1909 Mahler suffered the cruellest blows of his life in Vienna: the death of his elder daughter, a diagnosis of fatal heart disease, and his forced resignation as director of the Vienna Court Opera. Out of this double anguish he composed Das Lied von der Erde, setting German translations of Tang-dynasty Chinese poetry (Die chinesische Flöte) — turning verses by Li Bai, Wang Wei and others into an extreme meditation on life, death and parting.
mahler-das-lied-1909.meaning: Das Lied von der Erde is Mahler's ultimate farewell to Vienna and to life. The final movement, 'Der Abschied', runs half an hour and dissolves into silence on the word 'ewig… ewig' ('forever… forever'). It pours the Eastern beauty of impermanence into the Western symphony, creating one of the most moving meditations on death in all of music.

place.name: Mahler's Vienna residence (Auenbruggergasse 2)
place.note: Mahler completed Das Lied von der Erde while living here, about a 15-minute walk from the Upper Belvedere garden.

source.summary: Completed around 1909; posthumously premiered on 20 November 1911 in Munich, conducted by Bruno Walter.

listening.note: The final movement, 'Der Abschied' — best heard alone, late at night — to feel how that 'ewig… ewig' turns a life into an echo.

klimt-beethoven-frieze-1902.genre: Mixed-media frieze (casein paint, gold leaf, semi-precious stones)
klimt-beethoven-frieze-1902.context: In 1902 the Vienna Secession held its Fourteenth Exhibition in honour of Beethoven, and Klimt created this 34-metre-long frieze. Following the narrative of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony, it depicts humanity's longing for happiness, its struggle with hostile forces, and final redemption in art. At the centre of the exhibition stood Max Klinger's sculpture of Beethoven, with the frieze running around the surrounding walls.
klimt-beethoven-frieze-1902.meaning: The frieze fuses music, painting and architecture into one — a perfect realisation of the Secession's ideal of the 'total work of art' (Gesamtkunstwerk). With it Klimt declared that, in an age of collapsing values, art alone can grant humanity dignity. It is now permanently displayed in the basement of the Secession building, still radiating that fevered fin-de-siècle Viennese beauty.

source.summary: Created for the Fourteenth Secession Exhibition of 1902; mixed media (casein paint, gold leaf, semi-precious stones), 2.15 m high and 34 m around, now permanently displayed in the Secession's basement.

klimt-der-kuss-1908.context: Between 1907 and 1908 Klimt completed The Kiss (Der Kuss) in his Vienna studio. A couple kneel at the edge of a flowering meadow, both wrapped in golden robes, their faces hidden in the moment of a kiss — his profile clear, her eyes closed in rapture. Klimt used real gold leaf liberally, continuing the opulent style of his 'Golden Phase'.
klimt-der-kuss-1908.meaning: The Kiss is Klimt's most famous work and the visual emblem of fin-de-siècle Vienna. It compresses eros, death and artistic redemption into a near-square canvas, evoking at once ultimate solace and a tremor at the cliff's edge. After the Austrian state bought the painting and put it on permanent display at the Belvedere, Vienna became the only home of this 'most expensive kiss'.

place.name: Upper Belvedere, Vienna
place.note: The painting is held here; it was created in Klimt's studio, then on Josefstädter Straße.

source.summary: Completed around 1907–1908, oil and gold leaf, 180 × 180 cm; Austrian state property, permanently displayed at the Upper Belvedere in Vienna.

schiele-tod-und-maedchen-1915.context: In 1915, on the eve of being drafted, Schiele completed Death and the Maiden (Tod und Mädchen). A skeletal man (a self-portrait) clutches a limp, collapsing woman; her eyes are shut in surrender, his stare hollow and stubborn — a despairing embrace on the edge of life and death. Schiele was soon called up for the First World War, encountering real death at the front.
schiele-tod-und-maedchen-1915.meaning: Schiele tore apart the sensual aesthetics of Klimt's 'Golden Phase', replacing them with skeletal lines and the raw tension of life entwined with death. The painting is a central work of Austrian Expressionism and the most direct expression of fin-de-siècle Vienna's foreboding of war and death. Schiele died of the Spanish flu in 1918, aged just 28.

place.name: Upper Belvedere, Vienna
place.note: Many of Schiele's works are held here; this was created in his Vienna studio.

source.summary: Completed 1915, oil on canvas, 150 × 180 cm, now held at the Upper Belvedere in Vienna.

kokoschka-traeumende-knaben-1908.context: In 1908 the 22-year-old Kokoschka was commissioned by the Wiener Werkstätte to create the children's picture book The Dreaming Youths (Die träumenden Knaben), shown at the Vienna Kunstschau. With dreamlike colour and distorted figural lines, it hid metaphors of eros and death, provoking fierce backlash from Vienna's moral guardians — yet winning Klimt's admiration.
kokoschka-traeumende-knaben-1908.meaning: The Dreaming Youths announced the birth of Austrian Expressionism. With it Kokoschka declared war on the decorative aesthetics of the Vienna Secession, arguing that art should reveal humanity's most primal fears and desires rather than beautify them. This 'picture book' thus became one of the earliest Expressionist manifestos in the history of modern art.

place.name: Museum of Applied Arts, Vienna (MAK)
place.note: The Wiener Werkstätte was closely tied to the MAK; the book's exhibition and holdings are associated with this institution.

source.summary: Completed 1908; originally a colour-lithograph picture book dedicated to Klimt, it stirred controversy after being shown at the Vienna Kunstschau.

secession-building-1898.context: In 1897 Gustav Klimt and other artists broke from the conservative artists' association to found the 'Vienna Secession', declaring 'To every age its art, to art its freedom.' The following year the exhibition hall designed by Joseph Maria Olbrich was completed — a white cubic mass crowned by a golden openwork laurel dome that Viennese wits nicknamed the 'golden cabbage'.
secession-building-1898.meaning: The Secession building was the first full realisation of Otto Wagner's idea that 'modern architecture serves modern life'. Deliberately rejecting the piled-up ornament of historicism, it proclaimed through clean geometric forms and organic motifs that a new century needs new forms. Still used today as a contemporary-art exhibition space, it is the finest inheritor of the Secession's free spirit.

source.summary: The Secession was founded in 1897; designed by Olbrich and completed in 1898 at Friedrichstraße 12, fusing Art Nouveau with geometric form.

otto-wagner-karlsplatz-1899.context: In the 1890s Otto Wagner was commissioned to design a series of stations and pavilions for the Vienna Stadtbahn (city railway). The two symmetrical pavilions at Karlsplatz (around 1899) are his most representative masterpiece: a steel framework clad in white marble panels, the façades decorated with golden sunflower and plant motifs, perfectly fusing industrial functionality with the organic aesthetics of Art Nouveau.
otto-wagner-karlsplatz-1899.meaning: These two pavilions are a manifesto that 'modern architecture serves the modern city': urban infrastructure can be beautiful, and can express a city's culture rather than conceal it. The Viennese modernism Wagner pioneered directly influenced a generation of architects such as Adolf Loos and Josef Hoffmann, and foretold the functionalist architecture of the 20th century.

source.summary: Completed around 1899, a pair of pavilions at Karlsplatz; steel structure clad in marble panels with golden plant ornament, now one of the branch sites of the Wien Museum.

loos-haus-1911.context: In 1910–1911 Adolf Loos erected a commercial building on Michaelerplatz, directly facing the gate of the Hofburg, that did away entirely with ornamental window lintels. The Viennese were shocked by its bare façade and called it the 'house without eyebrows'; Emperor Franz Joseph is said to have ordered the curtains of the palace windows facing it drawn, refusing to look at the 'monster'. Loos held to his creed: ornament is crime (Ornament ist Verbrechen).
loos-haus-1911.meaning: The Looshaus is one of the decisive moments in Western architectural history: it directly challenged the millennia-old tradition that a building must 'beautify' itself, declaring that function is form. Its standoff with the Hofburg is the most dramatic confrontation between the modern spirit and the imperial legacy. It directly shaped the conceptual foundations of the Bauhaus and the whole modernist movement.

source.summary: Built 1910–1911 at Michaelerplatz 3; its unornamented façade caused an uproar, and Loos was forced to add flower boxes to defuse the controversy.

freud-hysterie-1895.context: In 1895 Freud co-authored and published Studies on Hysteria (Studien über Hysterie) with the eminent Viennese physician Josef Breuer, including the famous case of 'Anna O.' and several others. They proposed the 'talking cure': by letting patients freely recount repressed memories, their symptoms could dissolve. This was the starting point of psychoanalysis.
freud-hysterie-1895.meaning: Freud and Breuer together dug the first tunnel toward the unconscious. In a Vienna that prized reason and order above all, their work declared that the true drivers of human behaviour lie hidden beneath the surface of consciousness. This book changed how humanity understands itself.

place.name: Sigmund Freud Museum (Berggasse 19)
place.note: Freud lived and worked at Berggasse 19 for 47 years (1891–1938); his consulting room and study are preserved intact.

source.summary: Published 1895, co-authored by Freud and Breuer, containing five case studies that laid the clinical foundation of psychoanalysis.

freud-traumdeutung-1899.context: In November 1899 (post-dated by the publisher to 1900), Freud completed The Interpretation of Dreams (Die Traumdeutung) at Berggasse 19. In it he proposed that 'the dream is the royal road to the unconscious' and systematically set out core theories such as the Oedipus complex and wish-fulfilment. The first edition sold only 351 copies and took eight years to sell out, yet it would ultimately change the whole 20th century.
freud-traumdeutung-1899.meaning: The Interpretation of Dreams is one of the most subversive works in the history of modern thought. It declared that humanity's most private nocturnal visions are not random noise but the encoded expression of repressed desires and fears. Written in Vienna, it belongs to all humanity — it redrew the boundaries of the 'self'.

source.summary: The German edition appeared in November 1899 (dated 1900), first printing 600 copies, sold out over eight years; later translated into many languages and one of the most important books of the 20th century.

adler-nervoeser-charakter-1912.context: In 1911 Adler broke with Freud — stressing social and power factors over the sexual drive — and founded his own school of 'individual psychology'. The following year he published The Neurotic Character (Über den nervösen Charakter), arguing that the 'feeling of inferiority' and the 'striving for superiority' are the two core drivers of human behaviour. Adler led open discussions at venues such as the Café Central in Vienna, reaching a far wider audience than Freud's closed consulting room.
adler-nervoeser-charakter-1912.meaning: Adler brought psychoanalysis out of the private consulting room and into public society. He was a pioneer in applying psychology to education and community intervention, an important forerunner of modern positive psychology. His 'inferiority-and-transcendence' theory deeply shaped 20th-century educational philosophy and popular psychology, seeping into everyday language more broadly than Freud's.

place.name: Café Central, Vienna
place.note: The Café Central was a regular gathering place for Viennese intellectuals such as Adler, Kraus and Trotsky.

source.summary: The Neurotic Character was published in 1912, founding individual psychology; Adler led psychology discussions at the Café Central in Vienna.

wittgenstein-tractatus-1921.context: In 1921 Ludwig Wittgenstein — born into one of Vienna's wealthiest families — published the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus in the German journal Annalen der Naturphilosophie. This strange work, built from numbered propositions, ends with 'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.' Believing philosophy's task thereby complete, Wittgenstein resigned his Cambridge post and went off to teach in an Austrian village primary school.
wittgenstein-tractatus-1921.meaning: With its inquiry into the limits of language, the Tractatus opened one of the most important traditions of 20th-century analytic philosophy and directly inspired the logical positivism of the Vienna Circle. Wittgenstein's own Viennese roots — the palace he grew up in was decorated by Klimt — gathered in him the strange genes of both Secession aesthetics and analytic philosophy.

place.name: Former Wittgenstein family palace (Alleegasse 16)
place.note: Wittgenstein spent his childhood here; his father Karl Wittgenstein was an Austrian steel magnate, the palace hung with Klimt paintings. It now houses a media and cultural institution.

source.summary: The German text appeared in 1921 in Annalen der Naturphilosophie; the English translation, with an introduction by Russell, appeared in 1922 — hailed as one of the most important philosophical works of the 20th century.

vienna-circle-1929.work: Manifesto 'The Scientific Conception of the World: The Vienna Circle'
vienna-circle-1929.person: The Vienna Circle (Schlick, Carnap, Neurath, et al.)
vienna-circle-1929.context: Between 1924 and 1936 the physicist-philosopher Moritz Schlick led a weekly philosophy seminar at the University of Vienna — later known as the 'Vienna Circle'. Members included Rudolf Carnap, Otto Neurath and Hans Hahn. In 1929 they issued the manifesto 'The Scientific Conception of the World', proposing logical positivism: only logic and empirical science can produce meaningful knowledge, and metaphysics is a set of meaningless propositions.
vienna-circle-1929.meaning: The Vienna Circle was one of the most influential philosophical movements of the 20th century, bringing scientific methodology to the heart of philosophy and deeply shaping the Anglo-American analytic tradition. But in 1936 Schlick was shot dead by a Nazi-sympathising student, and members fled into exile in Britain and America, carrying the seeds of logical positivism into the world — Vienna's last export of ideas before the empire's end in 1938.

place.name: University of Vienna (main building)
place.note: The Vienna Circle's regular meetings were held mainly at the University of Vienna's mathematics institute.

source.summary: The 1929 manifesto 'Wissenschaftliche Weltauffassung' was published; core members included Schlick, Carnap, Neurath and Hahn. After Schlick's assassination in 1936 the Circle dispersed, most members emigrating to Britain and America.

schnitzler-leutnant-gustl-1900.context: On Christmas 1900, Schnitzler published the novella Lieutenant Gustl in the Neue Freie Presse — the first complete stream-of-consciousness narrative in German literature. The whole text unfolds as the inner monologue of an Austro-Hungarian officer over a single night, exposing his bombast, cowardice and pathological obsession with honour. The Austrian military was outraged and stripped Schnitzler of his reserve rank as an army doctor.
schnitzler-leutnant-gustl-1900.meaning: Schnitzler practised the depiction of the unconscious in literature even earlier than Freud. Taking the military honour code as its target, the story dissects the spiritual crisis of the Habsburg empire: a society held together by appearances, already hollow and corrupt within. Freud himself called Schnitzler his literary 'doppelgänger'.

place.name: Former residence of Schnitzler (Frankgasse 1)
place.note: Schnitzler ran a practice and wrote in Vienna's 9th district, only a few hundred metres from Freud's Berggasse.

source.summary: Published on 25 December 1900 in the Neue Freie Presse, the first stream-of-consciousness story in German; Schnitzler was stripped of his reserve rank for it.

karl-kraus-die-fackel-1899.context: In April 1899 the satirist Karl Kraus founded the magazine Die Fackel (The Torch) in Vienna. From its first issue until it ceased in 1936, this pamphlet (922 issues in all) was written almost entirely by Kraus alone, using sharp, caustic language to attack the corruption of the Viennese press, the Habsburg bureaucracy, and the outbreak of the First World War. His war drama The Last Days of Mankind (Die letzten Tage der Menschheit) ran to 800 pages and was deemed unstageable, fit only for a 'theatre on Mars'.
karl-kraus-die-fackel-1899.meaning: Kraus was Vienna's sharpest moral voice. In a city that masked an empire's decay with café elegance and waltzes, he insisted on using the precision of language to resist every lie. His critique of journalistic language reads astonishingly modern today — an essential antidote for every reader in the information age.

place.name: Near the Musikverein, Vienna (Kraus's residential area)
place.note: Kraus lived here for many years, a single street from Vienna's musical heart.

source.summary: Founded 1899, ceased 1936, 922 issues in all; the Austrian National Library has digitised all issues for free online reading.

hofmannsthal-lord-chandos-1902.context: In 1902 the 28-year-old Hofmannsthal published The Lord Chandos Letter (Ein Brief) in Vienna. This fictional letter has its protagonist — an Elizabethan English nobleman — confess to Sir Francis Bacon that he has permanently lost the ability to organise the world through language: words crumble in his mouth, dissolving into fragments and losing meaning. With it Hofmannsthal bade farewell to his earlier identity as a prodigy poet.
hofmannsthal-lord-chandos-1902.meaning: The Chandos Letter is one of the founding manifestos of modernist literature: language is no longer a transparent tool but a system with its own ruptures and limits. In epistolary form it voices the deepest anxiety of fin-de-siècle Viennese intellectuals — an old world is dissolving while a new language has yet to be born. The essay is widely regarded as a shared starting point for 20th-century literature and philosophy.

place.name: Burgtheater, Vienna
place.note: Hofmannsthal was closely tied to the Burgtheater and a central figure of Vienna's literary circle; though this piece appeared in a Berlin newspaper, it belongs to his Viennese period.

source.summary: Published on 18–19 October 1902 in the Berlin newspaper Der Tag; with it Hofmannsthal left behind his early identity as a lyric poet and turned to drama.

makart-festzug-1879.context: In April 1879 Hans Makart — the 'painter prince' — designed a grand historicist pageant for the silver wedding of Emperor Franz Joseph I and Empress Elisabeth: tens of thousands of citizens, guilds and artists in Renaissance costume processed along the newly completed Ringstraße, led on horseback by Makart himself.
makart-festzug-1879.meaning: This was the summit of Ringstraße-era historicist aesthetics and bourgeois self-display. The 'Makart style' (Makartstil) once dominated Viennese taste in interiors, painting and fashion; it was precisely the rebellion against such ornate decoration that, twenty years later, gave rise to the Secession and Viennese modernism.

place.name: Ringstraße (Ring Road)
place.note: The route of the 1879 pageant; Makart's legendary studio was on Gusshausstraße in the 4th district.

source.summary: April 1879, a historicist pageant designed for the silver wedding of Franz Joseph I and Elisabeth, held along Vienna's Ringstraße.

image.caption: Hans Makart, The Falconer (1880) — a representative work of the opulent Makart style

wiener-werkstaette-1903.context: In 1903 the architect Josef Hoffmann and the painter Koloman Moser founded the 'Wiener Werkstätte' in Vienna's 7th district, financed by the banker Fritz Waerndorfer. Growing out of Secession ideals, it championed the 'total work of art' (Gesamtkunstwerk): everything from architecture, furniture and tableware to jewellery and clothing was hand-crafted to a unified modern aesthetic.
wiener-werkstaette-1903.meaning: The Wiener Werkstätte carried the Secession's pure-art revolution into everyday life, profoundly influencing 20th-century modern design and the Bauhaus with its geometric, restrained and refined language. It embodied fin-de-siècle Vienna's ambition that 'beauty should permeate every object', and closed in 1932 under the weight of its high costs.

place.name: Former Wiener Werkstätte premises (Neustiftgasse)
place.note: The workshop's address at its founding; its design archive is now held at the Museum of Applied Arts (MAK).

source.summary: Founded in 1903 by Hoffmann and Moser at Neustiftgasse in Vienna's 7th district; closed 1932, its design archive now held at the Museum of Applied Arts (MAK).

klimt-adele-bloch-bauer-1907.work: Portrait of Adele Bloch-Bauer I (Woman in Gold)
klimt-adele-bloch-bauer-1907.genre: Oil, silver and gold leaf, c. 140 × 140 cm
klimt-adele-bloch-bauer-1907.context: Between 1903 and 1907 Klimt painted the portrait of Adele, wife of the Viennese industrialist Ferdinand Bloch-Bauer. The sitter sinks into a dazzling plane of gold and silver leaf, with only her face, shoulders and clasped hands floating above it — the most laboured work of Klimt's 'Golden Phase', taking four years and leaving over a hundred preparatory studies.
klimt-adele-bloch-bauer-1907.meaning: It is the epitome of the marriage between fin-de-siècle Vienna's upper Jewish bourgeoisie and avant-garde art. After the Nazi annexation of Austria in 1938 the painting was looted; long hung at the Belvedere and called 'Austria's Mona Lisa', it was the subject of a famous 2006 restitution case (the basis for the film Woman in Gold), returned to Adele's heir Maria Altmann and now held at the Neue Galerie in New York.

place.name: Upper Belvedere, Vienna
place.note: Created in Klimt's Vienna studio; on long-term display here from 1941 to 2006, then moved to the Neue Galerie in New York after its restitution.

source.summary: Completed c. 1903–1907, oil with gold and silver leaf, c. 140 × 140 cm; held at the Belvedere 1941–2006, now at the Neue Galerie New York after restitution.

schiele-die-familie-1918.context: In 1918 Schiele triumphed at the Vienna Secession's 49th exhibition, becoming overnight the leading figure of Viennese painting after Klimt. That same year he painted The Family: the artist crouching behind, a naked woman and a child before him. Yet the child in the painting was never born — that autumn the Spanish flu first took his wife Edith, six months pregnant, and three days later Schiele too died, aged just 28.
schiele-die-familie-1918.meaning: The Family became a posthumous work about 'a family that never took shape', and a full stop for Vienna's golden generation: in this single year of 1918, Klimt, Otto Wagner, Koloman Moser and Schiele all died in succession, and the creativity of fin-de-siècle Vienna collapsed along with the empire.

place.name: Upper Belvedere, Vienna
place.note: Held here; Schiele's solo Secession show took place in Vienna in 1918.

source.summary: Painted 1918 (unfinished), oil on canvas, c. 150 × 160 cm, now held at the Upper Belvedere in Vienna.
